mission.title: Sustainability meets technology
mission.quote: 10 years is the minimum for a bicycle – and for a laptop too!
mission.paragraph1: As a non-profit association, we have been changing the way technology is used since 2003. Our mission is clear: extend the lifespan of IT devices and reduce e-waste through repair, refurbishment and sustainable practices.
mission.paragraph2: In our premises – a former bank – we have created a meeting place where technology and sustainability come together. Our approach combines hardware recycling with open source software to create sustainable IT solutions for people and the environment.

items.question: What's the difference between a user account and a membership?
items.answer: A user account is all you need for the marketplace, IT help, and workshops — it's free. An association membership gives you voting rights and makes you officially part of the membership structure. Most users never become members — that's completely fine.

items.question: What do I actually get as a member?
items.answer: Voting rights on association decisions and a place on the official membership list. We don't promise exclusive perks — our services are open to everyone. You become a member because you want to support the association.

items.question: How does joining work?
items.answer: Fill in the form, transfer the annual fee — done. You're a member immediately. No waiting, no approval process.

items.question: How do I pay the annual membership fee?
items.answer: After joining, you'll see the bank details directly on the confirmation page. Bank transfer or TWINT.

items.question: Can I leave at any time?
items.answer: Yes. A simple informal email to the committee is all it takes. Annual fees already paid are not refunded.

meta.title: FAQ – Frequently Asked Questions
meta.description: Answers to common questions about Linux & Open Source, repair, sustainability and e-waste.

hero.title: Frequently Asked Questions
hero.subtitle: Our answers on Linux & Open Source, repair, sustainability and responsible use of resources – in the spirit of our mission against e-waste.

faq.notListed: Can't find your question? We're happy to help in person.

items.q: Do you only work with open-source software?
items.a: No. Open source is our preferred route – for its transparency, security and longevity. Where it makes sense, we also use or integrate proprietary software, always with a focus on usefulness and sustainability.

items.q: Why is open source important to you?
items.a: Open source strengthens self-determination and repairability. It reduces dependencies and makes it possible to use devices for longer – key levers against e-waste.

items.q: What does 'Right to Repair' mean to you?
items.a: We advocate for repairable hardware, documented processes and available spare parts. This extends the lifespan of devices and preserves resources.

items.q: Do you only work with Linux, or also Windows and macOS?
items.a: We work exclusively with Linux and open-source software. We don't install proprietary operating systems (Windows/macOS). Very rarely we make exceptions when a goal demonstrably can't be achieved with open-source tools.

items.q: Do you recommend Linux for everyone?
items.a: Linux is our standard because it's resource-friendly, secure and sustainable. If proprietary tools are absolutely required, we evaluate alternatives – and discuss rare exceptions openly.

items.q: Do you support migration to Linux or open-source alternatives?
items.a: Yes. From pilot projects to full roll-out, we guide individuals, nonprofits and businesses – including training and ongoing support.

items.q: Do you repair devices that weren't bought from you?
items.a: Yes. We repair laptops, PCs and peripherals from a wide range of manufacturers – regardless of where they were purchased.

items.q: Do you repair Apple devices?
items.a: Hardware, yes – provided spare parts are available and a repair makes sense. OS installations are Linux-only; we don't install macOS or Windows.

items.q: How long do repairs take, and what do they cost?
items.a: That depends on the fault and the parts needed. We'll provide a quote on request and keep you fully informed of your options.

items.q: Do you offer data recovery and secure data erasure?
items.a: Yes. We recover data to the best of our ability and securely erase storage media on request using recognised standards (e.g. open-source tools).

items.q: Can I just drop in without an appointment?
items.a: Absolutely. For complex cases or business appointments, a quick message in advance is helpful.

items.q: How do you concretely reduce e-waste?
items.a: Through repairs, component reuse, refurbishing and responsible recycling. We also advise on long-lasting setups and resource-efficient software.

items.q: Do you accept old devices?
items.a: Yes, provided reuse or responsible recycling is possible. We inspect devices on the spot and decide transparently whether they'll be reused or dismantled.

items.q: Do you use second-hand spare parts?
items.a: Where technically sensible, we use tested second-life parts. That saves resources and can cut costs – with no compromise on function.

items.q: Why refurbished instead of new?
items.a: Refurbished devices extend useful life, save CO₂ and raw materials. With the right upgrades, they're often more than good enough – at fair prices.

items.q: Do you stock refurbished devices?
items.a: Yes. We offer carefully checked and refurbished devices with warranty – transparently documented.

items.q: What is the REVAMPED certification?
items.a: REVAMPED marks sustainable, repairable builds with documented provenance, clear upgrade paths and quality assurance.

items.q: Is there a warranty on your devices?
items.a: Yes. Refurbished devices come with a warranty. We state the details transparently for each product.

items.q: How do you handle my data during repairs?
items.a: Confidentially and minimally. We only access data when needed for diagnosis, and protect storage media technically and organisationally.

items.q: Do you offer certified data erasure?
items.a: We offer secure erasure procedures with a record. Just let us know what level of security you need.

items.q: Do you offer workshops or training?
items.a: Yes. Topics include: Linux introduction, repair basics, sustainable IT, open-source tools – for beginners through to advanced users.

items.q: How can I get involved or support you?
items.a: As a volunteer, partner or donor. Sharing your knowledge is also very welcome – together we create real impact.

meta.title: CO₂ calculation & sources — methodology | {orgName}
meta.description: How {orgName} estimates CO₂ savings — formula, assumptions, sources. Transparency, not marketing numbers.
meta.ogDescription: Formula, assumptions, sources for every CO₂ number on the site.

hero.title: CO₂ calculation & sources
hero.subtitle: We show where every CO₂ number on this page comes from. Conservatively rounded, always with a linked source. A smaller honest number beats a big unsupported one.

formula.heading: The formula
formula.expression: CO₂ saved per device = device weight (kg) × emission factor (kg CO₂e/kg)
formula.note: Estimate per device class. Model-specific numbers (Apple, Dell) are more precise and will be added as we incorporate manufacturer PCFs into our inventory.

weights.heading: Weight per device category
weights.intro: These average weights feed the formula. We use conservative values from publicly available product data for the most common models.
weights.footer: For "Study (cited)" we use the direct value from the inputs table above rather than the rough weight-times-factor estimate. As we add more category-specific LCAs (Apple/Dell PER), more categories will move here.

limits.heading: Limits of this estimate
items: The numbers cover the manufacturing phase of a new device minus refurbishment effort. Energy use during operation, shipping and end-of-life are not included.
Actual savings depend on the counterfactual scenario: would the person otherwise really have bought a new device? We assume yes — but that isn't everyone's reality.
Electricity mix assumption: Swiss grid (~12 g CO₂e/kWh). In countries with coal-heavy mixes, refurbishment emissions would be higher.
We round display values to 5 kg (under 100 kg) or 10 kg (above) — spurious precision like "287.4 kg" implies certainty we do not have.

contribute.heading: Updates & contributing
contribute.intro: This methodology is updated continuously. Calculation source code and the data linked here:

cards.description: The Linux Terminal Server Project (LTSP) allows multiple users to work simultaneously on older computers by connecting them to a powerful server. This optimises resource use and extends hardware life.

repairhub.title: Curved lamp at the RepairHub
repairhub.story: A large curved monitor with a broken display became a workshop lamp. The repairers really appreciate the even, shadow-free light while working — and the installation is an eye-catcher that visitors notice right away.

laden.title: revamp-it shop & office
laden.story: In the shop's entrance area, two upcycled lamps replace the existing ceiling lights — customer reactions are consistently positive. In the office, another lamp has been running flawlessly for over a year.

werkraum4.story: Around 20 lamps have been in daily use for about a year. The broad, shadow-free lighting is ideal for hands-on work. Track record: a single power-supply failure — fixed with a simple swap; the LED backlighting gave no trouble.

recreazzz.title: RecreaZZZ washrooms
recreazzz.story: Two lamps in use for over a year — switched on and off with every visit. The frequent switching caused no problems at all, and the feedback collected via tally sheet is mostly positive.

ambossrampe.title: AmbossRampe storage
ambossrampe.story: The installation confirms storage areas as a sensible place to use them: the lamps, installed for about a year, work flawlessly.

meta.title: Lenovo L2251pwd — monitor to lamp | Monitor upcycling
meta.description: Step-by-step guide: disassemble a Lenovo L2251pwd monitor, remove the LCD, and bridge the electronics into a standalone lamp.

lenovo_l2251pwd.title: Lenovo L2251pwd
lenovo_l2251pwd.subtitle: Turn a 22-inch monitor into a lamp
lenovo_l2251pwd.metaLine: Model: Lenovo L2251pwd · Duration: about 1 hour · Difficulty: easy
lenovo_l2251pwd.credit: Guide by Daniel, RevampIT · Published 28 May 2026

safety.title: Safety first
safety.body: Never work on a live circuit. Take the necessary precautions before any functional test. If you're unsure: bring in an electronics colleague.

schematic.title: Circuit principle
schematic.body: Voltage divider: 5V (red) → 1 kΩ → backlight signal (yellow) → 1 kΩ → GND (black). The other pins (3.3V, additional signals) stay unconnected.

done.title: Done!
done.body: The monitor is now a standalone lamp with a fixed backlight. Hang it, stand it, or build it into your furniture setup.

feedback.title: Got a better idea? Let us know.
feedback.body: This guide is open and improves with corrections. If you've found a better bridge circuit, a brightness control, or a nicer mount — we'd love your contribution.

stages.title: Disassemble the monitor
stages.intro: The build takes only a few steps and is straightforward with a little patience.

stages.title: Remove the LCD
stages.intro: Take out the black panel (LCD); the white panel (backlight) stays in — it becomes the light surface.

stages.title: Bridge the electronics
stages.intro: At this point the device still works like a monitor with manual on/off. For lamp operation, we bridge the control electronics.

steps.title: Unclip the bezel
steps.body: From the back, use plastic spudgers to 'unclip' the outer bezel. Work carefully along the edge so no clips break off.

images.alt: Lenovo L2251pwd with the outer bezel removed
Close-up of the plastic clips on the monitor bezel

steps.title: Detach the electronics housing
steps.body: On the back, remove the 4 screws on the sides. The electronics housing can then be lifted off.

images.alt: Opened electronics housing on the back of the monitor
Detached electronics housing with the board exposed

steps.title: Expose the LCD electronics
steps.body: On the metal housing: undo the screws holding the electronics. Carefully open the clips along the metal frame with a screwdriver. Fold the metal frame open. Underneath you'll see the LCD (black panel) and the backlight (white panel). The LCD is also secured with adhesive strips — they peel off easily.

steps.title: Remove the LCD, close the frame back up
steps.body: Take off the black LCD panel. Reassemble the metal frame — the white backlight stays protected inside. From now on, that's your luminous surface.

images.alt: Monitor without the LCD — only the white backlight remains visible

steps.title: Bridge the control electronics
steps.body: Technically the device is still a monitor at this point — it has to be turned on and off manually. For lamp operation, the control electronics are removed and a bridge is placed on the remaining connector. The bridge is a voltage divider between 5V (red), GND (black), and the backlight signal (yellow): two identical resistors (~1 kΩ) between red↔yellow and black↔yellow.

images.alt: Removed control electronics of the Lenovo monitor
Soldered bridge made of two 1 kΩ resistors
Close-up of the voltage-divider bridge at the connector

produkt.title: Monitor → wall or ceiling light. No new device, no new plastic.
produkt.intro: We reuse three components that still work in the decommissioned monitor: the power supply, the LED backlight, and the housing. The result is a light fixture for offices, stairwells, and school buildings.

steps.title: Pick suitable devices
steps.body: Monitors with LED backlights have priority: lower energy use, higher light output, almost no additional material needed.

steps.title: Check eligibility
steps.body: Suitable: scratches, holes, pixel defects, broken image rendering. Not suitable: broken power supply, fractured housing.

steps.title: Install the standby bypass
steps.body: Many monitors drop into standby after a few minutes. The fix: a small voltage divider on the low-voltage side of the power supply, soldered onto a self-made connector. One instruction sheet per monitor type.

steps.title: Mount it
steps.body: Most models have the VESA MIS-D hole pattern on the back — off-the-shelf monitor wall/ceiling mounts fit directly. For wall mounting we use bed-frame bearing fittings; for ceiling mounting, a 3D-printed clip to prevent the monitor from sliding sideways.

photoGallery.title: Lenovo L2251pwd retrofit, documented 28 May 2026
photoGallery.intro: Six bench shots from May 2026, from the decommissioned source monitor to the glowing panel light.

items.caption: Source device: Lenovo Wide LCD Monitor, FCC/CE/RoHS label on the back. Made in Korea, Lenovo 2009.
Housing opened. Left: the plastic back cover. Right: the aluminium carrier plate with adhesive strips and original screw locations.
LCD panel removed. Visible: the exposed LED backlight and the control strip. The LCD layer is removed because, with no image content to display, it would only dim the light.
Electronics spread out: logic board (right), button board (left) and connecting cables. On most models the logic board stays in the housing — only a small power-supply modification is needed.

The standby bypass: two resistors as a voltage divider, soldered directly onto the connector going to the power-supply pin. This keeps the light permanently on instead of dropping into standby. Solution developed by Benjamin Ernst (ReCreaZZZ).
Finished result. The monitor glows as a uniform light panel, in its original housing, with no new plastic parts.

kunden.title: Who buys a wall or ceiling light made from a monitor?
kunden.intro: Stage 1 work and conversations with potential buyers point to four target segments — professional lighting procurement, not a designer market.

segments.label: B2B direct — firms with large office floors
segments.body: Banks, insurance companies, public administrations. Pilot installations in stairwells and ancillary spaces, then inclusion in the procurement catalogue.

segments.label: Furniture and interior fit-out companies
segments.body: Cooperation with Swiss interior outfitters who add upcycling lamps to their range — as a story and as a sustainable alternative to Asian mass production.

segments.label: Hardware stores (DIY chains)
segments.body: Like the Brilliant panels: via established retail channels. High marketing effort, tight margins — more of a scaling option for later.

segments.label: Real estate firms (renovation/new build)
segments.body: Direct use during renovations or new construction: upcycling lights instead of new products. Outreach via property managers and architecture firms.

wissenschaft.title: ZHAW project 132.351 INNO-EE — life-cycle assessment per ISO 14044.
wissenschaft.intro: Innosuisse is funding a comparative life-cycle assessment (LCA) by ZHAW. It compares the environmental performance of the upcycling lamps against off-the-shelf LED and fluorescent lamps across the full life cycle.

rows.label: Title
rows.value: Environmental performance of the re-use of monitors as light sources

rows.label: Research partner
rows.value: ZHAW Institute for Sustainable Development, School of Engineering, Winterthur

rows.label: Methodology
rows.value: Life-cycle assessment per ISO 14044, primary data prioritised, modelling in SimaPro

scope.title: What the LCA investigates
items: Use as wall and ceiling lights in office buildings, stairwells and schools
Focus on PC monitors with LED backlights
Modification levels: LCD removal only vs. standby bypass vs. LED-strip replacement vs. full power-supply swap
Light output comparison: monitor lamps vs. conventional light sources (with measurements)
Break-even points: at and up to what usage duration is the reuse ecologically worthwhile?

wissenschaft.history: Originally the scientific work was meant to be done by FHNW (Prof. Christoph Hugi, Institute for Ecopreneurship). FHNW had to step out due to capacity constraints. ZHAW took over the mandate in autumn 2025 via an Innosuisse Innoscheck.

ce.title: Strategy: confirm existing CE, don't re-certify.
ce.intro: The retrofit adds no new components that would affect the monitors' CE status. Wall and ceiling lights are also further away from people than monitors at a workstation and are not viewed directly.

items.label: Assessment by Barologic GmbH Winterthur

executiveSummary.title: Summary for decision-makers
paragraphs: Revamp-IT converts decommissioned LED monitors into wall and ceiling lamps — produced at social workshop Werkraum 4 (zsge foundation), with scientific support from ZHAW (LCA per ISO 14044, project 132.351 INNO-EE).

As of mid-June 2026: Swico Innovation Fund stage 2 is running (budget CHF 65,000 gross, CHF 15,000 Innosuisse to ZHAW). Twelve monitor models successfully retrofitted; five with documented standby solution. First fully photo-documented model guide (Lenovo L2251pwd) online since 28 May 2026.

Before the Swico final report (30.06.2026): reliable unit costs from the small series and findings from pilot installations are still pending. The ZHAW LCA report is being finalised (due 18.06.2026); results were presented to the project team at the end of May. Next public milestone: Swico presentation on 03.07.2026.

meta.title: Lenovo L2440pwC — monitor into a lamp | Monitor upcycling
meta.description: Step-by-step guide: take apart a Lenovo L2440pwC monitor, remove the LCD and reuse the backlight as a light panel.

lenovo_l2440pwc.title: Lenovo L2440pwC
lenovo_l2440pwc.subtitle: Convert a 24-inch monitor into a lamp
lenovo_l2440pwc.metaLine: Model: Lenovo L2440pwC · Time: approx. 1 hour · Difficulty: easy · Draft
lenovo_l2440pwc.credit: Guide by Bruno, RevampIT · Draft — not yet published

safety.title: Safety first
safety.body: Never work on a live device. Disconnect the monitor fully from the mains and wait 1–2 minutes before you start. Take the necessary safety precautions before any function test — if unsure, bring in an electronics colleague.

tools.title: Tools & materials
items: Two suitable resistors (per the circuit diagram)

stages.title: Take apart the monitor
stages.intro: Make room and a clean workspace. As you take it apart, keep the parts in their orientation (buttons down, as in the pictures) and use a magnetic screwdriver together with a screw container so nothing gets lost.

stages.title: Bridge the electronics
stages.intro: The control electronics are removed and the remaining connector bridged, so the panel stays lit permanently without any controls.

stages.title: Remove the LCD
stages.intro: Take out the black LCD pane — the diffuser and light layers beneath stay clean and undamaged; they provide the even light later on.

stages.title: Reassembly & function test
stages.intro: Put everything back together securely — and only then plug it into the mains.

steps.title: Open the housing
steps.body: Lay the monitor face down on a soft surface. Remove all the screws on the back — some may sit under plastic or rubber covers that can be lifted off with a thin flat-head screwdriver. Run the pry tool along the gap between the back shell and the frame to release the adhesive, and carefully lever the housing parts apart until the clips audibly release. Caution: near the control buttons, cables may be attached to the cover — don't pull sharply. Carefully unplug the cable between the frame and the electronics.

steps.title: Remove the inner covers
steps.body: Set the plastic parts aside and lay the screen flat. Remove all visible screws on the inner brackets (we counted 8). Carefully detach cables held down with tape or adhesive pads. Unplug the ribbon connector cable between the electronics and the LCD; the two cables to the corners of the screen can stay plugged in. Remove the smaller box on the right, unplug and unscrew the board with the USB ports underneath it (no longer needed). Finally, remove the larger housing — first take off the adhesive.

steps.title: Remove the control board and modify the connector
steps.body: Remove the right-hand control board (not the one where the power comes in) and set it aside — it's no longer needed. On the remaining connector, pins are bridged with resistors; the exact circuit diagram will follow once it's verified. Carefully cover all exposed metal parts of the resistors and contacts with insulating tape, and secure the modified connector with tape so it doesn't come loose. Then set it aside — the LCD panel is next.

steps.title: Remove the LCD panel
steps.body: Release the display module from the metal frame — it's held by the same clips as at the start; release it carefully, using a flat-head screwdriver if needed. Unplug the ribbon cable to the LCD control electronics if still connected. The LCD control electronics sit under a metal cover and may be glued down — release both from the metal surface. Carefully remove the LCD panel (the outermost dark layer); it's no longer needed. Then remount the metal frame. Note: keep the diffuser and light layers beneath as clean and undamaged as possible.

steps.title: Reassembly and function test
steps.body: Securely refasten all remaining components and screw the housing back together (some screws are no longer needed). Only after fully reassembling should you plug in the mains lead and check that the backlight lights up as intended. Caution: never run function tests on an open, live device unless proper safety precautions have been taken.

done.title: Done!
done.body: After a successful conversion, the backlight serves as a large-area light source: removing the LCD panel makes the light visible and the monitor can be used as a lamp or light panel. (Result photos to come.)

feedback.title: Got a better idea? Write to us.
feedback.body: This guide is open and thrives on corrections. If you've found a better bridging circuit, a brightness control or a nicer way to mount it — we'd love to receive your contributions.

stages.title: Take apart the monitor

steps.title: Open the housing and unplug the connector cable
steps.body: Lay the monitor face down on a soft surface and remove all the screws on the back. To remove the plastic frame, first take off the metal housing: remove all adhesive strips and undo the two screws at the top. Carefully remove the LCD panel (the outermost dark layer); it's no longer needed. Then remount the metal frame. Note: keep the diffuser and light layers beneath as clean and undamaged as possible.

steps.title: Reassembly and function test
steps.body: Securely refasten all remaining components and screw the housing back together (some screws are no longer needed). Only after fully reassembling should you plug in the mains lead and check that the backlight lights up as intended. Caution: never run function tests on an open, live device unless proper safety precautions have been taken.

items.question: What's the difference between a user account and membership?
items.answer: You need a user account for marketplace, IT help and workshops — it's free. Association membership gives you voting rights and officially makes you part of the membership. Most users will never become members — that's completely fine.

items.question: What do I actually get as a member?
items.answer: Voting rights on association decisions and a place on the official membership list. We don't promise exclusive benefits — our services are for everyone. You become a member because you want to support the association.

items.question: How does joining work?
items.answer: Fill out the form, transfer the annual fee — done. You're a member immediately. No waiting, no approval needed.

items.question: How do I pay the annual fee?
items.answer: After joining, you'll see the bank details directly on the confirmation page. Bank transfer or TWINT.

items.question: Can I leave at any time?
items.answer: Yes. An informal email to the board is sufficient. Already paid annual fees are not refunded.
